title: Hack and Slash
subtitle: A fast-paced action office game where players uncover villainy of price-hacking, built at Futuregames.

intro.heading: Blending narrative with fast-paced action gameplay
intro.body:
Hack and Slash is an action game set in an office environment where the player uncovers a price-hacking conspiracy. The project required balancing intense combat mechanics with a compelling narrative thread that motivated players to progress.

The UX challenge was twofold: create combat interactions that felt responsive and satisfying, while weaving narrative elements into gameplay without interrupting the flow of action.

role.heading: UX Researcher & Designer
role.body: I was responsible for user research, narrative UX, and playtesting. My focus was on how players understood the story through gameplay alone — without cutscenes or heavy text — and how combat mechanics could reinforce the narrative theme.

problem.heading: How do you tell a story during non-stop action?
problem.body:
Action games often struggle with the tension between narrative and gameplay. Cutscenes break the flow, but without narrative context, combat becomes repetitive. The challenge was finding ways to deliver story beats within the action itself.

The office setting was unusual for an action game. Players needed environmental cues to understand the world and their role in it, without relying on text-heavy exposition.
bullets: Balancing narrative delivery with uninterrupted action gameplay
Making an office setting feel like a compelling game environment
Communicating player objectives through environmental storytelling
Ensuring combat mechanics supported the narrative theme

approach.heading: Research-driven design with narrative integration
approach.body:
The process began with research into how action games handle narrative. Competitor analysis of games like Hotline Miami and Katana Zero revealed effective patterns for environmental storytelling in fast-paced contexts.

Narrative elements were mapped onto gameplay beats — story reveals tied to boss encounters, environmental clues scattered through level design, and UI elements that reinforced the corporate thriller theme.

cards.title: Narrative Design
cards.body: Developed a story structure that delivered key plot points through gameplay events rather than cutscenes — boss introductions, environmental clues, and progressive reveals.

cards.title: Playtesting
cards.body: Conducted playtesting sessions focused on narrative comprehension — tracking whether players understood the story and their motivations through gameplay alone.

outcome.heading: Narrative clarity through gameplay integration
outcome.body: Post-playtest analysis showed that players who engaged with environmental storytelling had significantly better narrative comprehension without any reduction in action engagement. The office setting, initially seen as a constraint, became one of the game's most distinctive features.
bullets: Players followed the narrative through environmental cues alone
The office setting became a unique and memorable game element
Combat mechanics and narrative delivery complemented each other

keyLearning.heading: Narrative and action don't have to compete
keyLearning.body: When integrated thoughtfully, story and gameplay reinforce each other. The biggest takeaway was that constraints — like an unusual setting — can become strengths if you design with them rather than against them.
